reason:
Both answers correctly land on "very worried," and both cover the same core actions: fast-detect/fast-repair (synthetic monitoring, remote config, resilient selectors), capturing users into a direct relationship, and diversifying acquisition. Technical specifics (ytd-* tags, remote config vs remote code policy, MutationObservers) are accurate in both.

B edges ahead on substance: it explicitly separates breakage risk from policy risk (CWS takedown/ToS enforcement) as a distinct, fatal failure mode, which is a real and important risk category A only mentions in passing. B also gives more concrete, decision-relevant framing — a numeric diversification target (<50% within 12 months), an explicit "model the dead-tomorrow scenario" exercise, and a closing pragmatic caveat not to over-rotate (don't rewrite everything, don't abandon a channel that works). This makes B's strategic section slightly more useful for a busy decision-maker, while the two are essentially equal on the technical resilience playbook.
